Title: A review of shape-morphing solutions and evolutional neural networks for spatiotemporal dynamics

URL Source: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.19526v1

Published Time: Mon, 23 Mar 2026 00:18:43 GMT

Number of Pages: 39

Markdown Content:
## A review of shape-morphing solutions and evolutional neural networks for spatiotemporal dynamics 

Mohammad Farazmand вҲ—

Department of Mathematics, North Carolina State University, 2311 Stinson Drive, Raleigh, NC 27695-8205, USA 

Abstract 

Shape-morphing solutions (SMS) refer to a class of approximate so-lutions of partial differential equations (PDEs) with the distinguishing feature that they depend nonlinearly on a set of time-dependent parame-ters. They generalize Galerkin truncations by allowing the basis (or trial functions) to evolve in time in order to adapt to the solution of the PDE. As such, SMS are particularly suitable for reduced-order modeling as well as high fidelity simulation of multiscale systems which exhibit localized time-dependent features, such as vortices, dispersive wave packets, and shocks. Furthermore, being mesh-free, SMS is scalable for solving PDEs in higher spatial dimensions. As a special case, SMS allows the approx-imation of the PDEвҖҷs solution by a neural network whose weights and biases depend on time. Such neural networks are known as evolutional neural networks or neural Galerkin schemes. The evolution of SMS pa-rameters is dictated by the SMS equation, a set of ordinary differential equations derived from the DiracвҖ“Frenkel variational principle. Over the past five years, contributions to the theory and computation of SMS have been growing rapidly. Here, we survey these developments, showcase some applications of SMS, and highlight important open problems for future re-search. At the same time, this review is structured to serve as a tutorial for applied mathematicians, physicist, and engineers who wish to enter this field.

1 Introduction 

Consider a general time-dependent partial differential equation (PDE),

The choice of the basis (or trial) functions П•i(x), which are inde-pendent of time, results in different numerical methods. For example, choosing Fourier modes as the basis leads to the Fourier (pseudo-) spectral method and piecewise linear (hat) functions lead to the finite element method. However, the static nature of the basis П•i may lead to computational in-efficiencies when the solution comprises time-varying localized structures. Ex-amples include vortices in fluid dynamics, shock waves, jet streams in the at-mosphere, and dispersive wave packets. For such PDEs, an unnecessarily large 2(a)

Shape-morphing solutions (SMS) address this issue by allowing the basis functions to also evolve over time, hence moving and deforming in order to adapt to the solution of the PDE (see figure 1, for examples). In applying SMS to a given PDE, several theoretical and computational questions immediately arise. For example, (i) what is the optimal shape of the SMS ЛҶ u(x, Оё ОёОё(t)) and its dependence on the parameters ОёОёОё? (ii) How does the approximation error depend on the shape of the SMS and the number of parameters n? (iii) How should the parameters ОёОёОё(t) evolve so that an SMS ЛҶu(x, Оё ОёОё(t)) best approximates the solution of the PDE? (iv) What computational 3methods should be used for efficient implementation of SMS? As we review here, some of these questions have been addressed (at least partially) and some remain open problems. The idea of SMS was introduced by Anderson and Farazmand [1] in the con-text of reduced-order modeling (ROM) and independently by Du and Zaki [31] in the context of neural networks. When SMS are used for reduced-order mod-eling, they are also referred to as Reduced-Order Nonlinear Solutions (RONS). When used as a neural network, they are called an Evolutional Deep Neural Network (EDNN). Ever since their introduction, there has been a fast growing body of work studying the theoretical and computational aspects of SMS. This review pa-per seeks to survey these developments and highlight potential areas for future research. 

1.1 Historical background 

Studies that are directly related to SMS are reviewed throughout this paper. In this section, we take the opportunity to give a historical overview of more conventional methods that seek to address the same underlying problems. In particular, we review adaptive mesh refinement, multigrid methods, and mesh-free solvers. Readers who are not interested in this historical background may safely skip this section. 

Adaptive methods : Adaptive methods use an evolving grid to adequately resolve all scales. A smaller grid size is used to resolve localized small-scale structures, whereas the large-scale surroundings are resolved using a larger grid size. Since small scale structures deform and move, the grid needs to be updated during time stepping. Adaptive methods are ubiquitously used in various scientific fields where multiscale PDEs arise, such as in ocean model-ing [18, 24, 71], meteorology [34, 66, 96], astrophysics [64], and fluid-structure interactions [8, 9, 10, 99]. A naive implementation of adaptive methods can be prohibitive since mesh generation at every time step is computationally expensive. Furthermore, spe-cial attention needs to be given to the time stepping scheme itself, especially for hyperbolic PDEs, so that it is stable across all grid sizes. These considerations have been addressed in a class of algorithms referred to as Adaptive Mesh Re-finement (AMR) methods which systematically refine the mesh in the regions where higher resolution is needed [12]. Adaptive grids can be used in conjunc-tion with familiar discretization methods such as finite difference [9, 11, 12], finite element [6, 7], and finite volume [24, 43, 104] schemes. More recently, even higher-order spectral/hp element methods have been used on adaptive grids [95]. Another class of adaptive methods, primarily used in fluid dynamics, is the so-called arbitrary Lagrangian-Eulerian (ALE) method [51, 52, 53, 98]. The main idea behind the ALE method is to move the grid with the fluid with the primary purpose of resolving localized small-scale structures and shock waves. This requires tracking the Lagrangian (or material) deformation of the fluid on 4an evolving grid in addition to its evolution on a fixed Eulerian grid. Although ALE is often used with finite difference or finite volume discretizations, it has also been adopted for use with spectral/hp element methods [46, 47, 57] We point out that the idea of changing the basis function is already implicit in adaptive methods. The evolution of the mesh can be recast as changing the basis over which the PDE solution is being approximated. Shape-morphing solutions make this notion explicit by optimally and automatically changing the trial functions as opposed to the grid. 

Multiresolution and multigrid methods : Wavelet methods were originally de-veloped for signal processing and data compression [28, 38, 44, 67, 81, 105]. Unlike the Fourier basis which is local only in the frequency domain, wavelets can be local in both space and frequency domains. It was quickly realized that wavelets can be used for analysis of multiscale phenomena beyond signal processing. For instance, wavelets were used to analyze coherent structures in two- and three-dimensional turbulence [60, 84, 92]. Wavelet-based meth-ods have also been developed to numerically solve multiscale PDEs, both with Galerkin truncation [14, 16, 17, 26, 27] and with collocation points [15, 61, 93]. Multiresolution analysis (MRA) provides a systematic method for construct-ing wavelets which from a basis for L2, and more general Hilbert or Banach spaces [28, 45, 77, 81, 101]. Derivation based on MRA yields a hierarchy of subspaces which correspond to ever smaller length scales that are localized in space. Note the resemblance to multigrid methods [19, 20, 33, 78, 86] which encode various scales in a hierarchy of grids as opposed to basis functions. It is important to note that wavelet methods still use a time-independent basis (or set of modes) for the function space. The number of wavelets grows exponentially with the resolution level (2 j for level j). Consequently, wavelet methods are quite memory intensive. Adaptive wavelet methods have been introduced to manage these memory issues [29, 61, 79, 80, 100]. In contrast, SMS evolves the basis over time to adapt to the solution of the PDE. As a result, far fewer modes are required alleviating the memory issues associated with wavelets. 

Mesh-free solvers : To avoid the computationally expensive steps of gen-erating and updating a computational grid, a variety of mesh-free methods have been developed. Examples include the smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) [40, 76], radial basis functions [56], diffuse element method [82], repro-ducing kernel particle method [73], and meshless local PetrovвҖ“Galerkin method [5]. Perhaps the most prominent among these methods is SPH which estimates a filed quantity as a convolution with a smooth kernel. These kernels can be interpreted as a weighted average of the contribution of each fluid particle to the field quantity of interest. The PDE dictates the distribution of these particles and their associated kernels. We observe that certain shape-morphing solutions can be viewed as a mesh-free method; for instance, see examples (7) and (8) in Section 3.1 below. 51.2 Outline 

The remainder of this review is organized as follows: 

вҖў Section 2 introduces a simple working example on which the SMS theory is demonstrated. 

вҖў Section 3 reviews the main theoretical results. 

вҖў Section 4 described a geometric interpretation of SMS and its implications. 

вҖў Section 5 elucidates the connections between SMS and evolutional neural networks. 

вҖў Section 6 presents four numerical examples, showcasing the applications of SMS. 

вҖў Section 7 discusses the computational aspects of SMS. 

вҖў Section 8 presents concluding remarks and future directions. 

2 Working example 

As we review in this paper, the SMS framework is applicable to general, nonlin-ear and challenging PDEs. However, it is helpful to consider a simple working example on which the theory and computational methods are illustrated. For such a working example, we choose the linear advection-diffusion equation,

Throughout this paper, we use this working example to illustrate theoretical and computational aspects of SMS. Carrying out the required calculations man-ually could be laborious. However, using a symbolic computing software such as Mathematica or Maple, the computations can be carried out within seconds. 

3 Shape-morphing solutions 

The PDE (1) is defined on a d-dimensional spatial domain x вҲҲ D вҠҶ Rd with the appropriate boundary conditions. The solution to the PDE is therefore a function u : D Г— R+ вҶ’ Rp. In the working example (4), we have d = 1, D = R,and the boundary conditions only require that the solution decays to zero at infinity, lim |x|вҶ’вҲһ u(x, t ) = 0. In this review, we only consider scalar-valued PDEs ( p = 1), but general-ization to higher dimensions is straightforward; see, e.g., [49]. Furthermore, we only consider strong solutions of the PDE. Generalization to weak solutions is not so straightforward. We emphasize that, although we consider scalar-valued PDEs, the spatial domain D is allowed to have a higher dimension d. In fact, the computational ef-ficiency of SMS, compared to conventional methods, becomes more pronounced as the dimension d increases; see Section 7.1. The distinguishing feature of SMS is that they depend nonlinearly on their time-dependent parameters. This motivates the following definition. 

Definition 1 (SMS) . We refer to ЛҶ u(x, Оё ОёОё(t)) as a shape-morphing solution of (1) if it solves the PDE and depends nonlinearly on at least one of its parameters 

Оёi.We denote the set of all admissible parameter values by О© so that ОёОёОё(t) вҲҲ

О© вҠҶ Rn. If ЛҶ u(x, Оё ОёОё(t)) approximates the solution of the PDE (instead of being an exact solution), we refer to it as an approximate SMS . However, since finding exact solutions of nonlinear PDEs are notoriously difficult, almost all solutions discussed in this review are approximate SMS, which we simply refer to as SMS.

Remark 1. Before discussing the solution to this minimization problem, a few technical remarks are in order. 1. Equation (13) is an instantaneous optimization problem in the following sense. For a given set of parameters ОёОёОё(t) at time t, Eq. (13) determines the rate of change of the parameters such that the norm of the residual at time t

3.3 Enforcing conserved quantities 

Certain PDEs are derived from conservation laws such as conservation of mass, momentum, energy, etc [70]. Others admit secondary conserved quantities that are discovered only after the PDE is formulated [69]. In either case, an approx-imate solution should ideally also conserve these quantities. This is specially important for reduced-order models [22] which do not approximate the solution accurately but need to preserve overall physical properties of the system. In the framework of shape-morphing solutions, it is straightforward to ensure that the ROM (or even a high-fidelity solution) respect the conserved quanti-ties of the PDE. This is accomplished by adding the conserved quantities as constraints to the minimization problem (13). 11 Definition 2. We refer to I : H вҶ’ R as a conserved quantity (or a first integral) of the PDE (1), if I(u(В·, t )) = I(u0) for all times t вүҘ 0 and (almost) all initial conditions u0.Evaluating a quantity I along an SMS ЛҶ u(В·, Оё ОёОё(t)), we can view the result as a function of the SMS parameters ОёОёОё(t). As such, for notational simplicity, we write I(ОёОёОё(t)) instead of I(ЛҶ u(В·, Оё ОёОё(t))). Our working example (4) does not admit a conserved quantity. But in the inviscid case ОҪ = 0, it is easy to verify that I = R вҲһвҲ’вҲһ |u(x, t )|2dx is conserved. Evaluating this conserved quantity at the SMS (6), we obtain

Since the right-hand side vanishes, the resulting Lagrange multiplier is О» = 0. As a result, in this special case, the constraint SMS equa-tion (27) coincides with the SMS equation. This is a consequence of the fact that the SMS equation (23) with ОҪ = 0 happens to automatically conserve the first integral (25) and therefore no further enforcement is required. 

Remark 3. In the constrained SMS (26), one should be cautious not to over-constrain the problem. For instance, if the number of constraints m surpasses the number of SMS parameters n, the constrained optimization problem may be ill-posed; in fact, the constraint set may even be empty. In other words, the model complexity n should be large enough to allow for the existence of an SMS which also respects the conserved quantities. 

Remark 4. Note that the constrained SMS equation (27) conserves the first integrals Ik in continuous time. However, after time discretization, the conser-vation may be lost, unless a conservative discretization scheme is used. Alter-natively, one can formulate and enforce the constraints at the discrete level as discussed by Schwerdtner et al. [94]. 

4 Geometric interpretation of SMS 

As discussed in [1], shape-morphing solutions have a nice geometric interpre-tation: SMS can be viewed as an n-dimensional submanifold of the functions space H.Consider the parameter set О©. An SMS can be viewed as a mapping ЛҶ u :

An evolution of parameters ОёОёОё(t) defines a curve in О©, which in turn defines a curve on the SMS manifold, corresponding to the SMS evolution ЛҶu(В·, Оё ОёОё(t)). In the special case of Galerkin approximation (2), where ЛҶ u depends linearly on the amplitudes Оұi, the manifold is flat and spans a linear subspace of the Hilbert space H.The SMS manifold M is somewhat unusual: although it is an n-dimensional manifold, it lives in the infinite-dimensional function space H; each point on this manifold is a function of x. 5 Neural networks as SMS 

In general, the appeal of neural networks stems from their universal approxima-tion property. For instance, Cybenko [25] showed that shallow neural networks with sigmoidal activation functions can approximate a continuous function with arbitrary accuracy. This result has been extended to deep neural networks and larger classes of functions such as Lebesgue spaces Lp [87, 55] and Sobolev spaces 

W k,p [54, 83]. Therefore, it is natural to try to approximate the solution of a PDE using a neural network. Although this viewpoint dates back to 1990s [68], it has gained renewed mo-mentum over the past decade with the introduction of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) [90, 58] and operator learning methods [72, 65, 74]. These methods learn the static network parameters (i.e., weights and biases) through numerical optimization, e.g., stochastic gradient descent. Du and Zaki [31] introduced an interesting and alternative viewpoint. They envisioned the network parameters as time-dependent variables whose values change as the solution of the PDE evolves. They refer to such networks as evolutional neural networks, also known as neural Galerkin schemes [21]. For example, Eq. (8) describes an evolutional shallow neural network with time-dependent internal weights wi(t), biases bi(t), and output weights Оұi(t). Non-linearities are encoded in the activation function Пғ. The network parameters therefore are ОёОёОё(t) = {Оұi(t), wi(t), b i(t)}ri=1 for a network with r hidden nodes. 15 Table 1: Examples of PDEs solved with SMS: Nonlinear Schrodinger (NLS), KuramotoвҖ“Sivashinsky (KS), NavierвҖ“Stokes (NS), FokkerвҖ“Planck (FP).

NS Пүt + v В· вҲҮ Пү = ОҪвҲҶПү Eq. (7) Decay d = 2

FP pt + вҲҮ В· (vp) = ОҪвҲҶp Eq. (7) Decay d вүҘ 2Figure 3 shows the architecture of this neural network. This shallow architecture can easily be generalized to deep neural networks. Let Nв„“ denote the output of the в„“-th layer of a deep neural network with L

hidden layers. The next layer is defined by the recursive relation 

Nв„“+1 = Пғ(Wв„“(t)Nв„“ + bв„“(t)) , (35) where в„“ = 0 , 1, В· В· В· , L , the time-dependent matrix Wв„“(t) determines the internal weights, and the vector bв„“(t) denotes the biases. For the input layer, we have 

N0 = x and the output layer is given by ЛҶ u(x, Оё ОёОё(t)) = WL+1 (t)NL+1 + bL+1 (t). Collecting all the parameters of the network, we have ОёОёОё(t) = {Wв„“(t), bв„“(t)}L+1  

> в„“=0

.Clearly, evolutional neural networks are a special case of SMS. It is therefore not too surprising that Du and Zaki [31], in search of evolution equations for the parameters of the network, also arrived at the SMS equation (16). Compared to earlier neural network-based methods, evolutional neural net-works have a number of advantages and an important drawback. Their main advantages are the following. First, since the evolution equations for the net-work parameters ОёОёОё(t) are explicitly known, evolutional neural networks do not require any data or training. Only the PDE being solved needs to be known. Second, because they do not require training, evolutional neural networks do not suffer from training or out-of-sample error. On the other hand, evaluating an evolutional neural network requires solving a system of nonlinear ODEs, i.e., the SMS equation. As we discuss in Section 7, this step can be time consuming. In contrast, although the training cost of PINNs and neural operators is high, their evaluation after training is very fast. 

6 Numerical examples 

In this section, we present numerical examples where SMS are used to solve four different PDEs as listed in Table 1: Nonlinear Schrodinger (NLS), Ku-ramotoвҖ“Sivashinsky (KS), NavierвҖ“Stokes (NS), and FokkerвҖ“Planck (FP) equa-tions. For the first two examples (NLS and NS), we will present reduced-order (i.e., low fidelity) solutions of the PDEs, whereas the last two examples (KS and FP) present full-order (i.e., high fidelity) solutions. Except for the KS equation which uses periodic boundary conditions, the other PDEs are defined on the infinite spatial domain with the assumption that 16 the solutions (and their derivatives) decay to zero at infinity. 

6.1 Nonlinear SchrВЁ odinger 

The Nonlinear SchrВЁ odinger (NLS) equation is a perturbative approximation of dispersive waves appearing in water waves as well as optics [102]. NLS is complex valued, u(x, t ) вҲҲ C, and its modulus |u(x, t )| coincides with the wave envelope. In particular, NLS has been used extensively to model and predict so-called rogue waves, i.e., waves of extreme amplitude [85, 32]. Here, we show that SMS can be used to approximate rogue wave solutions of NLS in a reduced-order setting. For the SMS we choose the Gaussian wave packet, ЛҶu(x, Оё ОёОё(t)) = Оұ(t) exp

{Пғ(t), v (t), ПҶ (t)} which denote the length scale, velocity, and phase of the wave, respectively. There is no exact solution of NLS that takes the form (36). However, certain solutions of NLS can be approximated by this SMS. The corresponding SMS equation (16) for the parameters ОёОёОё = [ Оұ(t), Пғ (t), v (t), ПҶ (t)] вҠӨ simplifies to ЛҷОұ = вҲ’ 2Оұv Пғ , ЛҷПғ = 4 v

Figure 1(a) shows the SMS starting from the initial conditions Оұ(0) = 0 .2, 

Пғ(0) = 20, v(0) = вҲ’0.05, П•(0) = 0. This mimics a rogue wave in the sense that the wave starts from a relatively small amplitude at the initial time t = 0, grows to a relatively large amplitude (roughly twice the initial amplitude), and then decays back to a lower amplitude wave. To compare this reduced-order SMS against a direct numerical solution (DNS) of NLS, we examine the amplitude of the wave as a function of time. This amplitude coincides with the modulus of the solution at x = 0, i.e., |u(0 , t )|.Figure 4 shows this comparison, highlighting that the SMS reproduces the am-plitude and the timing of the maximum wave height with reasonable accuracy for a reduced-order solution. This figure also shows a ROM obtained from re-ducing NLS to four POD modes. We choose four POD modes to ensure fair comparison with the SMS which has four parameters. For further discussion on the application of SMS to dispersive waves, we refer to Anderson and Farazmand [2] who investigated SMS reduced-order solutions of NLS and modified NLS (a more accurate model of water waves). Among other things, they showed that the SMS solution remain accurate even when the wave groups propagate in space. Their theoretical results also revealed intricate connections between the SMS equation and the Lagrangian formulation of NLS. Finally, we refer to Zhang et al. [103] who applied SMS to the Gardner equation, a higher-order approximation of dispersive waves, in two spatial dimensions. 

6.2 NavierвҖ“Stokes 

The NavierвҖ“Stokes equation, modeling fluid flow, has already been solved us-ing SMS. For example, Du and Zaki [31] use evolutional deep neural networks to solve the incompressible NS equation in two spatial dimensions with peri-odic boundary conditions. More recently, Kim and Zaki [63] also solved the compressible Couette flow in 2D with no-slip boundary conditions. Here, we showcase an application of SMS to the so-called leapfrogging phe-nomenon in vortex dynamics. Consider a 2D flow which is initialized as in figure 5(a): four vortices of the same strength but opposite signs arranged in a square configuration. It is well-known that this configuration exhibits a leapfrogging motion, whereby the trailing vortices speed up, deform, and pass in between the leading vortices. This motion repeats indefinitely. We show that a simple SMS, consisting of four Gaussians reproduces this leapfrogging motion. Consider the vorticity formulation of NS (see Table 1) in the inviscid case ОҪ = 0. (a) (b) (c) (d) Figure 5: Leapfrogging dynamics of vortices reproduced by an SMS with n = 16 parameters. For the SMS, we consider a stream function ЛҶПҲ(x, Оё ОёОё(t)) consisting of r = 4 Gaussians as in (7). The parameters of this SMS are the amplitudes Оұi(t) вҲҲ R,lengthscales Пғi(t) вҲҲ R and the centers ОјОјОјi(t) вҲҲ R2, resulting in rK = 16 pa-rameters ОёОёОё(t) = {Оұi, Пғ i, Ој ОјОјi}ri=1 . These parameters are evolved according to the constrained SMS equation (27). The first integrals enforced in these equations are the kinetic energy I1(ПҲ) = R |вҲҮ ПҲ|2dx and enstrophy I2(ПҲ) = R |вҲҶПҲ|2dx.Figure 5 shows the resulting evolution of the SMS which qualitatively re-produces the leapfrogging motion. Anderson and Farazmand [1] compare this evolution against point vortex dynamics and report an excellent quantitative agreement as well. To reproduce the leapfrogging vortex dynamics using SMS only a 16 dimen-sional ODE system (SMS equation) needs to be formed and solved. It is worth pausing here and contemplate how this compares to conventional methods. To discretize the PDE, e.g. using finite differences or finite elements, one first needs to define an artificial boundary to contain the computations to a finite spatial domain. This domain needs to be large enough, with appropriate boundary conditions, to avoid unduly influencing the vortex dynamics [88]. Even then, to resolve the solution, the discretization will need to have far more degrees of freedom than the 16 parameters needed for SMS. Alternatively, imagine using POD modes to obtain a reduced-order solution. This approach would need to contend with two issues. First, a set of solutions of the vortex dynamics need to be obtained somehow from which the POD modes are extracted. Second, since the vortices advect to the right during their evolution, a set of globally defined static modes will struggle to accurately capture their dynamics. POD meth-ods are generally known to flounder when applied to such advection-dominated flows [91, 49]. 

6.3 KuramotoвҖ“Sivashinsky 

The KS equation was originally derived to model reaction fronts. We consider this PDE on a spatial domain x вҲҲ [вҲ’L/ 2, L/ 2] with L = 22 and periodic bound-ary conditions. For the SMS, we use the evolutional shallow neural network (8) with a hyperbolic tangent activation function, Пғ(В·) = tanh( В·). 6.4 FokkerвҖ“Planck 

The FokkerвҖ“Planck equation describes the evolution of the probability density function (PDF) associated with a stochastic differential equation (SDE). Con-sider the SDE, dX = v(X)d t + вҲҡ2ОҪ dW, X(0) = X0, (40) where v : Rd вҶ’ Rd is a sufficiently smooth vector field, W(t) вҲҲ Rd is the standard Wiener process and ОҪ > 0 is the diffusion coefficient. Because of the noise d W, the state of the system X(t) вҲҲ Rd is also random and therefore needs to be understood probabilistically. The probability density p(x, t ) of the state satisfied the FP equation in Table 1. The initial condition p(x, 0) = p0(x) is prescribed by the distribution of the initial state X0 which itself can be random. In the special case where the initial state X0 is known deterministically, the initial probability density is a Diract delta, p0(x) = Оҙ(x вҲ’ X0). As an example, we consider the stochastic Duffing oscillator, ВЁx = x вҲ’ 0.2 Лҷ x вҲ’ x3 + вҲҡ2ОҪ ЛҷW , (41) with ОҪ = 1 /40. Here, the state, X(t) = [ x(t), Лҷx(t)] вҠӨ, is two-dimensional ( d = 2). In the deterministic case ( ОҪ = 0), the damped Duffing oscillator (41) has two asymptotically stable fixed points X = ( Вұ1, 0) to which almost all trajecto-ries converge. In the stochastic case, trajectories still tend to converge to a neighborhood of these equilibria such that the probability density p(x, t ), in the asymptotic limit t вҶ’ вҲһ , is bimodal with its peaks near ( Вұ1, 0). However, the transient dynamics can be quite complicated and depends on the initial density 

p0(x). Figure 1(c) shows the evolution of an SMS corresponding to the FP equation of the stochastic Duffing oscillator. The SMS is a sum of r = 30 axisymmetric 21 Gaussians (7). The initial condition p0(x) is prescribed with 15 Gaussians cen-tered at (1 , 1) and the remaining 15 Gaussians at ( вҲ’1, вҲ’1). Figure 1(c) shows this initial density and its progression through time according to the SMS equa-tion. The Gaussians, which form the SMS, move and change shape until an asymptotic equilibrium is reached. By time t = 50, the SMS has reached its asymptotic state. Anderson and Farazmand [4] compared the SMS against large scale Monte Carlo simulations of the SDE (41). They observed excellent agree-ment, not only for the asymptotic equilibrium density, but also the transient dynamics of the density. In terms of computational time, it is notable that the Monte Carlo simulations take over two hours, whereas the SMS simulations take only about thirteen seconds. In this example, the parameters of the SMS are evolved according to the constrained SMS equation (27), where the first integral, 

I(ОёОёОё(t)) =

ЛҶp(x, Оё ОёОё(t))d x = 1 , (42) is enforced, ensuring that the SMS (7) is in fact a probability density function. Enforcing this constraint, in addition to ensuring that the results are physically meaningful, helps reduce the approximation error. For example, figure 7 shows the error for eight interacting particles in a harmonic trap (see [3, Section 4.1] for details). If constraint (42) is enforced in the SMS dynamics, the relative error remains under 10 вҲ’6. However, if the constraint is neglected, the error grows over time. This example highlights the importance of enforcing the conserved quantities of the PDE in the evolution equation of the corresponding SMS (see Section 3.3).

7 Computational aspects 

In this section, we review some computational bottlenecks of SMS as well as methods for addressing them. The main contributors to the computational cost of SMS can be divided in two categories: (i) formation cost which refers to the computation of the metric tensor M (ОёОёОё) and the right-hand side vector field f (ОёОёОё), and (ii) integration cost which refers to the numerical integration of the SMS equation in time. Sections 7.1 and 7.2 deal with the formation cost and Section 7.3 addresses the integration cost. In addition, we discuss the enforcement of boundary conditions of the PDE in Section 7.4. 

7.1 Symbolic SMS 

Regarding the formation cost, note that evaluating the metric tensor M and the right-hand side vector field f requires the computation of O(n2) inner prod-ucts (17) on the function space H. Each inner product requires the evaluation of an integral on the spatial domain D; see, e.g., the working example in equa-tion (20). Since these quantities depend on ОёОёОё(t), they need to be recomputed as the parameters evolve. The formation cost is not a major issue when SMS is used for reduced-order modeling since the number of parameters n is rela-tively small. On the other hand, for high-fidelity solutions where the number of parameters n is large, the formation cost becomes significant. However, for a large class of SMS, the number of required inner product computations can be drastically reduced to O(K2) where K вүӘ n. This is feasible if (i) the SMS takes the spectral form (3), and (ii) the inner products can be computed symbolically in closed form, e.g. using Mathematica or Maple. All examples introduced in Section 3.1 satisfy condition (i), but the evolutional 

deep neural network (35) does not. If the above conditions are met, the SMS has n = rK parameters, where r

is the number of terms in the summation (3) and K вҲ’ 1 is the number of shape 23 parameters in each term, ОІОІОІi = [ ОІi1, ОІ i2, В· В· В· , ОІ i(KвҲ’1) ]вҠӨ. If the inner products in SMS are obtained via symbolic computing, we refer to the method as symbolic SMS .The resulting symbolic expressions allow us to only compute a fraction of the terms in the metric tensor and the right-hand side vector filed. More specifically, as shown in figure 8(a), the metric tensor has a partitioned matrix structure with 

K Г— K blocks M (i,j ). Symmetry of the metric tensor dictates M (i,j ) = M (j,i ).Furthermore, each block itself is symmetric, [ M (i,j )]вҠӨ = M (i,j ). To evaluate all components of the metric tensor M , only a generic block M (i,j ) needs to be computed symbolically. The entries of this block consists of the inner products,

Once these symbolic expressions have been obtained, the entire metric tensor M can be evaluated by inserting the numerical values of Оұi, Оұj , ОІik , etc. A similar reduction in computational cost is applicable to the right-hand side vector field f : Rn вҶ’ Rn. As shown in figure 8(b), this vector field has r

blocks of size K Г— 1. The components of the i-th block, f (i), consist of the inner products,  вҲӮ ЛҶuвҲӮОұ i

, (45) where k вҲҲ { 1, В· В· В· , K вҲ’1}. As long as this generic block is computed symbolically, the entire right-hand side vector field f can be evaluated by direct substitution of the numerical values of the corresponding parameters Оұi and ОІik , etc. In summary, evaluating the metric tensor M and the vector field f require 

K(K + 3) /2 symbolic computations. This leads to two major computational savings. First, the number of required symbolic computations is independent of the number of terms r in the SMS. Second, the symbolic expressions need to be computed only once. As the time integration progresses, the updated numerical values of the parameters can be inserted in the previously computed symbolic expressions. The independence of the computational cost for symbolic SMS from the number of terms r has significant consequences. As r increases, so does the accuracy of the SMS approximation, but the formation cost remains constant. This allows symbolic SMS to be scalable for high-fidelity solutions. For example, the shape-morphing Gaussians (7), which were used to solve the NavierвҖ“Stokes equation in Section 6.2 and the FokkerвҖ“Planck equation in Section 6.4, have three shape parameters ( K = 4) and therefore only require 14 symbolic com-putations, regardless of the number of Gaussians used. This property enabled [4] to solve the FokkerвҖ“Planck equation accurately in higher spatial dimensions (d = 8), which would have been impractical for conventional methods that rely on spatial discretization. We refer to [3, Section 3.2] for a more detailed discussion of symbolic SMS. 24 7.2 Collocation SMS 

Obtaining symbolic expressions for the SMS equation is not always feasible. For example, Mathematica fails to return symbolic expressions for the metric tensor associated with the shallow neural network (8) with a hyperbolic tangent activation function. In such cases, a collocation version of the SMS equations can be formulated which does not require any functional inner product computations. To describe this collocation SMS method, consider the residual (11). Instead of minimizing the norm вҲҘR(В·, Оё ОёОё, ЛҷОёОёОё)вҲҘH, we minimize the mean squared residual at prescribed collocation points. More specifically, let {x1, x2, В· В· В· , xN } denote the collocation points. We seek to minimize the mean squared residual, min

M cij (ОёОёОё) = вҲӮ ЛҶuвҲӮОё j

collocation SMS equation .Note that the collocation metric tensor M c is an N Г—n matrix. Viewing (47) as a linear equation for ЛҷОёОёОё, it may or may not have a solution. If it does have a solution, the residual R vanishes at the collocation points along the correspond-ing SMS ЛҶ u(В·, Оё ОёОё(t)). If it does not have a solution, the collocation SMS equation must be interpreted as a least squares problem. In either case, the evolution of parameters is given by ЛҷОёОёОё = [ M c(ОёОёОё)] +f c(ОёОёОё). The advantage of the collocation SMS is that it can be formulated by the pointwise evaluations (48); it does not require any inner product (i.e., integral) evaluations. On the other hand, it may be less accurate since it only minimizes the residual at the collocation points instead of the residual over the entire spatial domain. Nonetheless, in practice, if the number of collocation points 

N is large enough, the resulting SMS is quite accurate. For example, the KS equation in Section 6.3 was solved using collocation SMS with N = 128. 

Remark 7. There is an intricate relationship between the SMS equation and the collocation SMS equation: under certain conditions, the collocation SMS equation (47) is equivalent to the Monte Carlo approximation of the SMS equa-tion (16). 25 For the collocation SMS, if N > n and the columns of M c are linearly inde-pendent, then [ M c]+ = ([ M c]вҠӨM c)вҲ’1[M c]вҠӨ. 7.3 Regularized SMS 

Now we turn to the computational challenges regarding the time integration of SMS equations. The (collocation) SMS equation can be stiff as a set of nonlinear differential equations. As a result, explicit time integration schemes, e.g. RungeвҖ“Kutta, need to take exceedingly small time steps. This stiffness is related to the condition number of the metric tensor M . When the number of SMS parameters n is small, such as the reduced-order solutions discussed in Sections 6.1-6.2, the metric tensor is often well conditioned and time integration is not an issue. However, as number of parameters increases, the metric ten-sor becomes more poorly conditioned and the resulting SMS equations exhibit hallmarks of stiffness. An obvious resolution is to use an implicit integration scheme [75]. Although implicit schemes allow for larger time steps, they require solving a nonlinear system whose computational cost and convergence need to be taken into account. If one insists on explicit time integrators, a straightforward remedy was proposed in [3] by adding a regularization to the minimizing problem (13). The regularized optimization problem reads, min 

> ЛҷОёОёОё

вҲҘR(В·, Оё ОёОё, ЛҷОёОёОё)вҲҘH + P ( ЛҷОёОёОё), (50) where P : Rn вҶ’ R+ is the regularization (or penalty). For example, choos-ing the Tikhonov regularization, P ( ЛҷОёОёОё) = ОівҲҘ ЛҷОёОёОёвҲҘ2, the resulting regularized SMS equation is given by (M (ОёОёОё) + ОіI) ЛҷОёОёОё = f (ОёОёОё), (51) 26 where I denotes the identity matrix and Оі is the regularization parameter. This regularization idea can be similarly applied to the collocation SMS (46). We refer to [3] for a detailed derivation. The regularized metric tensor M + ОіI is much better conditioned than the metric tensor M . Consequently, explicit time integration, within reasonable computational time, becomes feasible. However, the regularized SMS introduces a free parameter, i.e., the regularization parameter Оі, in the equations (51). As discussed [3], finding the optimal value of this regularization parameter is non-trivial. Alternatives to direct regularization (50) have also been explored. For ex-ample, Finzi et al. [35] use the randomized NystrВЁ om preconditioning [37] to achieve a better conditioned metric tensor M . This involves first obtaining a NystrВЁ om approximation Mnys of the metric tensor M and a subsequent precon-ditioning PвҲ’1Mnys of this approximation. The preconditioner P also contains a free parameter, a small regularization parameter whose optimal value needs to be specified. Another alternative to regularization was proposed by Berman and Pe-herstorfer [13]. Motivated by the observation that the collocation metric tensor 

M c is low-rank, at each time step, they only update a subset of the parameters 

ОёОёОё, leaving the rest unchanged. This is accomplished by defining a sparse param-eter vector ЛҷОёОёОёs = SвҠӨ ЛҷОёОёОё, where S is a sketching matrix, i.e., a subset of the columns of the identity matrix. More precisely, S = [ ei1 |ei2 | В· В· В· | eik ] вҲҲ RnГ—k where ei are the standard basis on Rn and the indices {i1, В· В· В· , i k} are a randomly selected subset of all possible indices {1, 2, В· В· В· , n } with k вүӘ n.The collocation metric tensor is accordingly modified to M c(ОёОёОё)S. This pro-cedure effectively modifies the collocation SMS equation (47) to M c(ОёОёОё)SS вҠӨ ЛҷОёОёОё =

f c(ОёОёОё). Note that this resembles a right preconditioning, although the orthogonal projection SS вҠӨ is not invertible. According to Berman and Peherstorfer [13], this randomized projection approach remedies overfitting and reduces the com-putational cost. They test their method on three PDEs (AllenвҖ“Cahn, Burgers, and Vlasov equations) and report a few orders of magnitude reduction in the relative error. 

7.4 Boundary conditions 

One of the major issues that remains to be addressed for SMS is the imposition of the boundary conditions of the PDE. LetвҖҷs denote the boundary conditions by 

Bu(x, t ) = 0 , вҲҖx вҲҲ вҲӮD, (52) where B is an appropriate boundary condition operator. For instance, for homo-geneous Dirichlet boundary conditions, B is the restriction of u to the boundary. For homogeneous Neumann boundary conditions, we have Bu = вҲӮu/вҲӮn , the directional derivative orthogonal to the boundary. Generalization to inhomoge-neous boundary conditions is straightforward. 27 Unlike classical discretization methods, such as finite elements and finite differences, where boundary conditions can be easily enforced, a clear and rig-orous methodology for imposing arbitrary boundary conditions for SMS remains illusive. Nonetheless, some workarounds have been developed. For example, the idea of positional embeddings was pursued by Du and Zaki [31] as well as Kast and Hesthaven [59]. In this framework, one defines a map ОЁ ОЁОЁ : D вҠӮ Rd вҶ’ Rde , where 

de is the dimension of the embedding space. An intermediate SMS U (y, Оё ОёОё(t)) is first defined on this embedding space where y вҲҲ Rde . Setting y = ОЁ ОЁОЁ( x), we obtain the final SMS, ЛҶu(x, Оё ОёОё(t)) := U (ОЁ ОЁОЁ( x), Оё ОёОё(t)) вҲ’ U (0, Оё ОёОё(t)) . (53) For example, equation (38) is an example of such positional embedding for periodic boundary conditions. In the context of evolutional neural networks (see Section 5), the positional embedding appears as an additional layer between the input layer and the first hidden layer of the network. To ensure that the desired boundary conditions (52) are satisfied by the SMS (53), the embedding ОЁ ОЁОЁ needs to satisfy certain conditions. In particular, consider the conditions, Dirichlet: ПҲi(x) = 0 , (54a) Neumann: вҲҮПҲi(x) В· n(x) = 0 , (54b) for all x вҲҲ вҲӮD and i = 1 , 2, В· В· В· , d e. Here, ПҲi denote the components of ОЁОЁОЁ = [ ПҲ1, В· В· В· , ПҲ de ]. If ПҲi satisfy (54a), then the SMS (53) satisfies the ho-mogeneous Dirichlet boundary condition. Similarly, if ПҲi satisfy (54b), then the SMS satisfies the homogeneous Neumann boundary condition. Obviously, the constant functions ПҲi вүЎ 0 satisfy conditions (54). However, they lead to a constant SMS (53), oversimplifying the spatial dependence of the SMS. Therefore, the positional embeddings ПҲi need to have enough variability inside D to obtain a non-trivial function ЛҶ u. In order to achieve such embedding functions, in the context of Dirichlet boundary conditions, Du and Zaki [31] used a function that depends on the distance of any point x вҲҲ D to the boundary 

вҲӮD. But their technique could not handle Neumann boundary conditions. Kast and Hesthaven [59] pursued a more general framework which can tackle both Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions. They proposed using the eigenfunctions of the Laplace operator, вҲҶПҲi = О»iПҲi, x вҲҲ D ,

BПҲi = 0 , x вҲҲ вҲӮD. (55) Other than limited cases, where these eigenfunctions are known analytically, they need to be obtained numerically, e.g., using a finite element method. This preprocessing step adds to the computational cost of the SMS and also impedes its applications in higher spatial dimensions. Furthermore, as Kast and Hes-thaven [59] point out, the embedding space dimension de has to be large enough to ensure adequate spatial variability of the resulting SMS inside D.28 For rectangular domains, Hilliard and Farazmand [50] introduced a domain doubling technique which allows for enforcement of Dirichlet, Neumann, and mixed Dirichlet-Neumann boundary conditions. We illustrate this method here for a one-dimensional domain [0 , L ] and homogeneous Dirichlet boundary con-ditions. First we double the domain size by considering the enlarged domain [вҲ’L, L ], on which we define the periodic embedding, 

ПҲ(x) = sin 

 ПҖx L + c(t)



. (56) Note that this function is periodic over the enlarged domain [ вҲ’L, L ]. Next, we consider the SMS, ЛҶu(x, Оё ОёОё(t)) = U (ПҲ(x), ЛҶОёОёОё(t)) вҲ’ U (ПҲ(вҲ’x), ЛҶОёОёОё(t)) , (57) where the continuous function U (В·, ЛҶОёОёОё(t)) is an intermediate SMS and ОёОёОё(t) = 

{ЛҶОёОёОё(t), c (t)} are the parameters of the SMS. It is easy to verify that ЛҶ u is 2 L-periodic and an odd function of x. These two properties ensure that ЛҶ u(0 , Оё ОёОё(t)) = ЛҶu(L, Оё ОёОё(t)) = 0 for all times t. Therefore, the SMS automatically satisfies the Dirichlet boundary conditions on the original domain [0 , L ]. We refer to [50, Appendix B] for generalization of this idea to higher dimen-sions as well as mixed Dirichlet-Neumann boundary conditions. This domain doubling technique is limited to rectangular domains and suffers from the same curse of dimensionality as the positional embeddings. We conclude this section by referring to Chen et al. [23] who seek to en-force the boundary conditions by treating them as constraints. This is similar to enforcing the first integrals of the PDE in the constrained SMS equation (see Section 3.3). However, this approach carries some subtle pitfalls that are discussed in [48, Section D.1.2] 

8 Conclusions and future directions 

As we reviewed here, the theoretical and computational aspects of shape-morphing solutions have rapidly advanced over the past five years. In this section, we re-view the main open problems and computational challenges that remain to be addressed in the future. 

Boundary conditions: As reviewed in Section 7.4, a few methods have been proposed for enforcing boundary conditions in SMS. However, the existing meth-ods are far from being satisfactory or computationally optimal. For example, the positional embeddings require a user-specified embedding dimension de with no clear theoretical guidelines for choosing this dimension. On the other hand, the domain doubling technique of [50] is not applicable to arbitrary geometries. Furthermore, both methods are restricted to relatively low spatial dimensions (d вүӨ 3). Therefore, developing a universal, unambiguous, and computationally efficient method for imposing boundary conditions remains an open problem. 

Shape of SMS: Perhaps the most nontrivial open problem is the choice of the SMSвҖҷs shape for a given PDE. In the context of evolutional neural networks, the 29 shape of the SMS is determined by the architecture and the activation functions of the network. In Section 6.2, we chose a sum of Gaussians to approximate vortex dynamics. This is a natural choice since vortex patches locally resemble a Gaussian [97]. In contrast, the rogue wave SMS (36), which was motivated by previous studies on optical waves, is quite nontrivial. In general, the optimal choice for the shape of the SMS is not clear. Here, вҖҳoptimalвҖҷ can be interpreted as the shape that approximates the PDE, within a desired accuracy, with the smallest number of parameters n. This optimality is particularly important when SMS is used for reduced-order modeling, since ROMs need to have a minimal number of degrees of freedom. But even for high-fidelity solutions, the optimal shape is important. Al-though most studies do not clearly state this, solving PDEs in lower dimensions (d вүӨ 3) using SMS often remains computationally more costly than using con-ventional grid-based methods such as finite elements or finite differences. This is mainly due to the fact that a non-optimal choice of the SMS forces us to use too many parameters to satisfactorily approximate the PDEвҖҷs solution, leading to unnecessarily large set of SMS equations. Furthermore, a judiciously chosen SMS shape can reduce the stiffness of the SMS equations and the condition number of the metric tensor, hence reducing the computational cost of temporal integration (see Section 7.3). To date, no systematic method has been proposed for determining the optimal SMS shape for a given PDE. 

Data assimilation with SMS: In academic settings, one is usually content to solve a PDE from an arbitrary or user-defined initial condition u0. How-ever, if the PDE is being used to forecast the system that it models, the initial condition cannot be arbitrary; it must be inferred from the available observa-tional data. This is the realm of data assimilation which is required for any numerical solver, including SMS. Hilliard and Farazmand [50] have developed a sequential data assimilation method which intermittently corrects the SMS pa-rameters ОёОёОё(t) based on observational data. For example, figure 6(b) shows the results of this data assimilation applied to the KS equation. The observations are pointwise measurements of the solution u(xi, t ) at 10 equispaced sensors at the points {xi}10 

> i=1

. This data is assimilated into the SMS solution for the first 30 time units. The SMS forecasts the system for the remaining 70 time units without data assimilation. As it is evident from the figure, the data assimilation significantly delays the growth of errors. Although these results are promising, there is much more to be done on this front. For example, Kalman filtering, variational data assimilation, and nudging methods can in principle be used in conjunction with SMS. However, the nonlinear dependence of SMS on its parameters ОёОёОё complicates a straightforward application of these methods, hence requiring new theoretical and computational developments. 

Weak solutions: All shape-morphing solutions discussed here seek to approx-imate strong (sufficiently smooth) solutions of PDEs. However, if the SMS is a non-smooth function of space (e.g., the shape-morphing finite elements (10)) or if the PDE solution itself is non-smooth, a weak formulation of SMS needs to 30 be used. Kim et al. [62] have begone work on weak formulation of SMS in the context of evolutional KolmogorovвҖ“Arnold networks. Such a weak formulation can be obtained by requiring that вҹЁR(В·, Оё ОёОё, ЛҷОёОёОё), v kвҹ©H =0, i.e., the residual (11) vanishes when evaluated against a set of test functions 

vk. It remains to identify a set of test functions for a given SMS that ensure well-posedness of the inner products вҹЁR(В·, Оё ОёОё, ЛҷОёОёОё), v kвҹ©H. Furthermore, an optimally chosen set of test functions may lead to a sparse and well-conditioned metric tensor which will ameliorates the subsequent temporal integration of the weak SMS equations. These theoretical and computational questions remain to be addressed. 

Error estimates: In spite of the extensive body of work on SMS reviewed here, there are virtually no rigorous results that quantify their approximation error [39]. The universal approximation theorem is usually invoked to argue that as the number of parameters n increases, the SMS will evermore closely approximate the true solution. Although this argument is correct, rigorous error estimates will specify the rate at which the error decays. Furthermore, error estimates will help avoid overfitting and may inform the choices we make about the shape of the SMS. This need is especially acute when SMS are used for model order reduction, where a minimal number of degrees of freedom is desirable. Hence, I conclude this review with an invitation to mathematical analysts to take this exciting and novel problem on.
